segments.s424-g9: شنید is a past simple verb 'he heard' placed before its object for poetic word order. این سخنها is the object phrase 'these words' (این = demonstrative + سخنها = plural 'words/speeches'). یل اسفندیار is the subject in apposition 'the hero Esfandiyar' following the object, a common flexible word order in classical Persian.
segments.s424-g10: پیاده is an adverbial/adjunct 'on foot'. بیامد here is a past/perfective 'he came' (classical/poetic form) describing the subject from the previous segment. بر is the preposition 'to/toward' and نامدار is an epithet 'renowned' (the person he approached); the whole phrase is a prepositional phrase governed by the verb بیامد.
segments.s424-g11: به رستم چنین گفت is 'he said thus to Rostam' — the prepositional object (به رستم) precedes گفت (said) and چنین ('thus') reports the manner. کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing someone directly, and سرگرای is a descriptive vocative 'you who are preoccupied/occupied' (an adjective/agentive form used in address).
segments.s424-g12: چرا is the interrogative 'why' introducing the question. تیز is an adjective/adverb 'swift/quick', and گشتی is a 2nd-person past/perfective verbal form 'you went/you became/you came' (here: 'you have come/hastened'); the pronoun is omitted but understood from address. به پرده‌سرای is a prepositional phrase 'to the curtained hall/the stage/tent-palace' indicating destination.
segments.s424-g13: سزدگر is an agentive phrase 'one who deems it fitting' (a topical noun phrase). برین بوم is a locative expression 'on this soil/land' where بر = 'on' and ین is the poetic/orthographic form of 'این' ('this'), and زابلستان names the region (in apposition after 'this land').
segments.s424-g14: نهد is a classical 3rd-person singular verb 'he places/sets' (here hypothetical or general: 'would set/place'). دانشی is the object 'a knowledge' (indefinite/abstract), and نام غلغلستان is a noun phrase 'the name "Ghalghalestan"' (i.e. the knowledge given that name); the verb governs the following noun phrase as its object.
segments.s424-g15: که introduces a subordinate clause ('for/when'). مهمان چو سیر آید از میزبان literally 'when the guest comes sated from the host' — چو + verb is a temporal 'when', سیر is verbal/adjectival here meaning 'sated', and آید is the present/habitual/past classical 'comes'. از indicates origin 'from' and میزبان is 'the host'.
segments.s424-g16: به زشتی is an adverbial phrase 'in an ugly/shameful manner' or 'to disgrace'. برد here functions as a verb 'he takes/carries' (classical usage 'he carries off' or 'takes away') and نام پالیزبان is the object 'the name/reputation of the steward/keeper' — together 'he carries off the steward's name in disgrace.'
segments.s424-g1: چو is the classical conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. رستم is the subject name. بدر شد is a two-word verbal phrase: بدر (here poetically 'to appear/come out') + شد (past 'became'/'happened') yielding 'came out/appeared'. ز + پرده‌سرای is the prepositional complement 'from the curtained chamber' (ز = from, پرده‌سرای = curtained room/stage). Word order follows older Persian: temporal marker + subject + verbal phrase + prepositional complement.
segments.s424-g2: زمانی همی بود is a short stative clause: زمانی 'for a while', همی is the older continuous/emphatic particle, بود is the past copula 'was' meaning here 'he stood/was (for a while)'. بر در is a locative phrase 'at the door/gate' (بر = on/at, در = door), and به پای is an idiomatic locative 'on foot'/'standing on his feet' (به = to/at, پای = foot). The sequence places the temporal phrase before the copula as is common in classical verse.
segments.s424-g3: به کریاس گفت is a simple clause 'he said to Kriyas' (به = to, کریاس = proper name, گفت = said). The following ای سرای امید is a vocative phrase 'O house/abode of hope' addressing the addressee; ای is the classical vocative particle. In older Persian the reported speech and vocative may be arranged with the verb before or after the address.
segments.s424-g4: This is the content of the utterance: خنک روز 'a cool/fresh day' (adjective + noun), کاندر = 'in which/wherein' introducing a subordinate relative clause, تو is the internal object 'you', بد (here adjectival/adverbial) indicates ill-treatment or harm, and جمشید is the agent 'Jamshid'. Syntactically the relative adverb کاندر links 'خنک روز' to the clause 'Jamshid did you harm'.
segments.s424-g5: همایون بدی گاه combines contrasting adjectives before گاه 'time/occasion': همایون 'auspicious', بدی 'bad/ill-omened', producing a rhetorical contrast. کاوس names the king associated with that time (Kay Kavus). کی here functions as an interrogative marker ('was it for...? / which was it?'), so the phrase asks whether the time belonged to Kay Kavus and whether it was auspicious or calamitous.
segments.s424-g6: همان روز 'that same day' is resumed and identified with کیخسرو نیک‌پی 'Kay Khosrow the well-born/virtuous' — a nominative phrase placing the epithet نیک‌پی after the name. The clause contrasts the previous possibility (Kay Kavus's time) with the day of Kay Khosrow.
segments.s424-g7: در فرهی is a locative phrase 'in the noble hall/court' (در = in, فرهی = noble/courtly). بر تو اکنون ببست is a causative/result clause: بر تو 'against/over you' (here 'upon you' or 'up against you'), اکنون 'now', ببست is the classical past of بستن 'he shut/locked/bound' (third-person past, here 'they shut/locked you up'). The clause thus says someone has now confined/imprisoned you in the hall.
segments.s424-g8: که introduces the explanatory subordinate clause 'because/that': بر تخت تو 'upon your throne' (بر = on, تخت = throne, تو = your), ناسزایی modifies the occupant as 'unworthy/unsuitable' (an epithet applied to the newcomer), and نشست is the past 'sat/settled' (i.e. 'an unworthy one sat upon your throne'). The subordinate explains the reason for the earlier confinement.
segments.s424-g97: چگونه کنم = a rhetorical question: چگونه (how) + کنم (1st-person present/subjunctive of کردن used here to mean 'can I do / shall I do'). ترس را is the direct object marked by را (definite/object marker). از دلم = a prepositional phrase: از (from) + دل (heart) + the enclitic possessive -م (my). The word order is typical Persian: interrogative phrase → verb → object → source phrase.
segments.s424-g98: بدین سان is a formulaic adverbial phrase meaning 'in this way / thus' (here treated as a unit). کز introduces a subordinate clause ('that / so that'); اندیشه‌ها is the plural object 'thoughts'. بگسلم is 1st-person present/subjunctive/optative of گسستن used figuratively 'I break off / separate myself' — it expresses the intended result of the action in the main clause. Together the clause means 'so that I may break away from my thoughts.'
segments.s424-g99: This is an enumerative noun phrase. جنگی here is an adjective/noun meaning 'warlike person / fighter' and is counted (دو implicit before it); شیر = 'lion'; و = 'and'; مرد = 'man' (here 'man' as heroic figure); دلیر = adjective 'brave' modifying مرد. The sequence lists participants: 'two fighters, two lions, and two brave men.'
segments.s424-g100: چه دانم = 'how/what do I know' — a rhetorical formula (چه + دانم). The following clause is introduced by که (that/which): پشت = 'back' (the physical back, here meaning 'being turned over / defeated'); the second که functions as an interrogative/relative 'which/who'; آید به زیر = verb phrase 'comes down / is brought under' (آید = comes, به زیر = under/beneath). Overall: 'how can I know which back will come under' i.e. 'who will be defeated.'
segments.s424-g101: ورا = 'to him / to that one' (indirect object marker + pronoun). نامور = adjective 'famed, renowned' describing the person. هیچ پاسخ نداد = literal 'gave no answer': هیچ (not any) modifies پاسخ (reply), and نداد is past tense of دادن 'gave' (here negative: 'did not give'). Word order is indirect object → epithet → negated object phrase → verb.
segments.s424-g102: دلش = دل (heart) + enclitic possessive -ش (his/her) = 'his heart'. گشت is past/past-subjunctive of گشتن used here 'became'. پر درد = 'full of pain/sorrow' (پر = full (of) + درد = sorrow). و connects clauses. سر پر ز باد = 'head full of wind' (سر = head; پر = full; ز = poetic from/of; باد = wind), a poetic idiom meaning his head was filled with empty/fickle thoughts. The two clauses describe his inner state after the unanswered address.
segments.s424-g89: This is a short reporting clause: proper name (پشوتن) + indirect object (بدو, “to him”) + past-tense verb (گفت, “said”) followed by a vocative (کای نامدار). کای is an archaic/vocative particle equivalent to English “O,” and نامدار is a predicate adjective addressing the addressee (“renowned”). Word order is Subject – Indirect Object – Verb, then the vocative.
segments.s424-g90: This is an inverted, poetic clause meaning “you say so much about the battle.” such/so (چنین) + degree/question word (چند, “how much”) + verb-form (گویی) here functions as 2nd-person present ‘you say’ (an older, non‑periphrastic present instead of modern می‌گویی). The subject pronoun تو appears after the verb for emphasis, and the prepositional phrase از کارزار (“about/of the battlefield”) marks the topic.
segments.s424-g91: This subordinate temporal clause begins with که (introducing explanation/reason) + تا used temporally (“when/once”), then the 2nd-person past رسیدی (“you reached/attained/took up”). The preposition به marks the things attained (به تیر و کمان = “bow and arrow” as a compound object). The clause gives the time reference for what follows.
segments.s424-g92: This is an archaic word order: the negative past copula/verb نبد (an older form meaning “there was not / did not exist”) appears first, followed by the prepositional phrase بر تو (“upon you”) and then the topical/genitive marked phrase ابلیس را این گمان. Here را marks ابلیس as the possessor/topic of the following noun (این گمان = “this thought/suspicion/temptation”), so the whole means “this thought of the Devil was not upon you.”
segments.s424-g93: Literal order: prepositional phrase of direction (به دل, “into/to the heart”) + object with topic marker (دیو را, “the devil” + را) + noun راه (“way/path”) + past second‑person verb دادی (“you gave/you allowed”) and then the temporal adverb کنون (“now”)—so: “you have given the devil a way into your heart.” را marks دیو as definite/topic; راه دادی is an idiom meaning to allow entry or opportunity.
segments.s424-g94: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle (here ‘now/still/indeed’) and نشنوی is the present 2nd‑person negative (ن + شنوی) meaning “you do not hear/heed.” The object is پند این رهنمون (“the advice of this guide/this guidance”), with این rhematic/ demonstrative modifying رهنمون. Together: “now you will not heed the counsel of this guide.”
segments.s424-g95: دلت is دل + the 2nd‑person possessive clitic -ت, ‘your heart.’ خیره is an adjective ‘staring/astonished,’ and بینم is 1st‑person present ‘I see.’ The clause then continues with همی (still) + پر ستیز, where پر is a prenominal adjective meaning ‘full (of)’ and ستیز is ‘strife/fighting.’ Word order (possessed noun → adjective → verb) and the clitic are typical of poetic inversion: “I see your heart astonished, still full of strife.”
segments.s424-g96: کنون (‘now’) starts the clause; هرچ (‘whatever/whatever I have’) + گفتم (past first person ‘I said’) gives the content scope, and همه ریزریز literally means ‘all in pieces/bit by bit.’ Rیزریز is an adverb/adjectival phrase meaning broken up or scattered, so the sense is that everything the speaker has said has been scattered or come to nothing.
segments.s424-g17: سراپرده را places the addressee/object (the inner curtain/inner chamber) before the verb and is marked by را, the definite/direct-object (or topical) marker. گفت is the simple past 3rd-person singular 'said' (here treated as the speaking verb). بد روزگار is the agent/subject 'ill‑fate' or 'bad time' following the verb; the word order (object + verb + subject) is a poetic/archaic ordering allowed in classical Persian.
segments.s424-g18: که introduces reported speech ('that' / 'that which'); جمشید را is the definite object marked by را. داشتی is the 2nd-person singular past imperfect/past ('you had' / 'you used to have') addressing the curtain; بر کنار is a prepositional phrase 'at/upon the side' meaning 'at the side of' or 'beside'. Together this clause reports what Ill‑fate said.
segments.s424-g19: همان روز is a temporal phrase 'that same day' (emphatic). کز بهر is an archaic compound where کز ('that/which' or 'from which') introduces the reason/purpose and بهر means 'for/on account of' — together 'which was for' or 'because of'; کاوس شاه is the name with the title following (reverse apposition common in classical Persian: 'Kavus the king').
segments.s424-g20: بدی here functions as a noun 'harm/ill' (literally 'badness') that befalls something; پرده و سایه بارگاه is a coordinated object: 'the curtain and the shadow/canopy of the palace' (سایه + بارگاه = 'the palace's shade/protection'). The phrase states that harm befell those parts of the royal household.
segments.s424-g21: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. راه یزدان is a genitival phrase 'the way/path of God' (یزدان = God). همی is an archaic/prosodic particle used with verbs to mark ongoing or habitual action; بازجست is the past/preterite 'sought' (or 'sought again'), so the clause asks rhetorically where God's way was to be sought.
segments.s424-g22: همی again marks the continual or emphatic aspect. خواستی is 2nd-person past 'you wished/attempted' and here has the sense of 'you tried/endeavoured'. اختران را is the object 'the stars' (note: the provided sense list for اختران does not include 'stars', so -1 is used). درست is a predicative/adjectival idea 'right/correct' or a resultative 'to make right' — together 'you tried to set the stars right.'
segments.s424-g23: زمین زو is a compressed poetic phrase: زمین 'the land' and ز (here enclitic + pronoun: زُ/از او) meaning 'from him' or 'because of him' — so 'the land, because of him'. سراسر پرآشوب بود is the predicate 'was entirely tumultuous' (سراسر = throughout/entirely, پرآشوب = tumultuous, بود = past copula 'was').
segments.s424-g24: پر از expresses 'full of' and takes از plus a list of items. خنجر و غارت و چوب is a coordinated list 'daggers and plunder and clubs/sticks' and بود is the final copula applying to the whole phrase ('was full of ...'). The repetition of و links the enumerated items; in classical Persian such lists commonly end with بود as the copula.
segments.s424-g25: کنون is a sentence adverb meaning “now” and sits at the front to time the whole clause. مایه‌دار (literally “holder of mâyé”) is a noun meaning a provider/supporter and is followed by the possessive pronoun تو (your); Persian allows the possessor pronoun after the noun (مایه‌دارِ تو). The clause closes with the copula گشتاسپ است = “is Gushtasp.”
segments.s424-g26: به پیش وی اندر is a locative prepositional phrase (به پیش = before / in the presence of; وی = him; اندر = within/inside) giving the place. چو here is a poetic particle meaning “as/like” or introducing the following name, and جاماسپ است is a simple nominal clause with the copula (“Jamasp is”), so the whole segment says that Jamasp is present in his presence/in attendance.
segments.s424-g27: نشسته is a past participle/adjectival verb meaning “seated” and functions as a predicate. به یک دست is a locative phrase literally “at one hand/one side” (دست used as ‘side’), followed by the personal pronoun او “him,” and then the name زردهشت (Zarathushtra). Word order is predicate – locative – possessor – name, common in older poetic Persian.
segments.s424-g28: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause modifying زردهشت (“who ...”). با is the preposition “with.” زند is an older/poetic noun here (written as زند) presented as an accompaniment (treated as a noun proper). واست is an archaic/poetic particle bound into the verbal phrase; آمدست is the perfective verb “has come,” and از بهشت gives the source “from paradise.” Grammatically this is a relative clause: “who, with X, has come from heaven.”
segments.s424-g29: به دیگر پشوتن is a prepositional phrase “to the other Peshotan” (به = to; دیگر = other; پشوتن = proper name). گو is the imperative of گفتن “say/tell” addressed to the vocative نیک‌مرد (“O good/noble man”), which follows in direct address. The order (indirect object phrase – imperative – vocative) is typical of Persian poetic diction.
segments.s424-g30: چشیده is a perfect/past participle meaning “having tasted/experienced” and functions as the predicate describing the person referenced. ز is the poetic preposition “from/of,” گیتی is “the world,” بسی is an adverb meaning “much” or “many,” and گرم و سرد (“hot and cold”) are concrete nouns used idiomatically for hardships/extremes. The phrase thus means he has experienced many of the world’s heats and colds.
segments.s424-g31: به پیش اندرون is a locative phrase meaning roughly “in the inner forecourt / before (them) inside” (به پیش = before/in the presence of; اندرون = inner part/within). فرخ is an epithet meaning “fortunate/auspicious” that precedes the proper name اسفندیار; Persian often places adjectives before the name in poetry.
segments.s424-g32: کزو is a relative/adverbial compound meaning “from whom / by whose presence.” شاد شد is a two-word verbal expression where شد is the past of شدن (“became”) and شاد is the predicative adjective “happy/joyful” — together “became joyful.” گردش روزگار is a nominal phrase “the turning/cycle of time” (گردش = turning/cycle; روزگار = time/fate). Grammatically: “from whose coming the course of time became glad.”
segments.s424-g41: یکی here means 'a certain' (introducing one instance). ژنده is an adjective 'ragged' placed before the noun پیل 'elephant'. است is the copula 'is' linking the noun phrase to the locative phrase that follows. The locative بر + کوه گنگ ('on/upon the (mute/Gung) mountain') comes after the copula; گنگ functions as an adjective modifying کوه (here treated as epithet of the mountain).
segments.s424-g42: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). با سلیح is an instrumental/comitative phrase 'with a weapon'. اندر آید uses the archaic/pre-classical preposition اندر + the present/subjunctive form آید ('he comes / should come'), meaning 'enters/comes into'. به جنگ is the goal/locative 'into battle' (به here = 'into/toward'). The whole clause sets a hypothetical: 'if he comes into battle with a weapon...'.
segments.s424-g43: This is another conditional clause: اگر 'if' + با سلیح 'with weapons'. نبردی is a verbal-nominal form meaning 'a fight / fighting' (here 'if there were fighting' or 'if he engaged in combat'); its precise morphological reading is archaic/poetic and functions as the verbal/noun complement. بود is the copula/auxiliary in the past/conditional sense 'would be' (so the clause yields a counterfactual or general conditional).
segments.s424-g44: همانا is an emphatic 'verily' (marks assertion). که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. آیین here means 'custom, code' (not ritual), and مردی means 'manliness' or 'the ways of a man' rather than a single man; بود again is the past/definitive copula 'was/would be' completing the predication 'that would be the code of manliness.'
segments.s424-g45: به بالا 'upward' (به here = toward/into + بالا = up). همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle meaning 'continually/indeed' used in epic diction. بگذرد is the present/future-oriented third-person singular of 'to pass' (here 'he will pass'). فر is the technical epic noun 'farr/royal glory' and زیب means 'beauty' or 'adornment'; the two nouns are coordinated by و and form the direct object/thing he passes with ('passes with glory and beauty').
segments.s424-g46: بترسم is the first-person present/future-tense form meaning 'I fear' or 'I am afraid' (the prefixed بـ is an archaic present/indicative marker). که introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...'. فردا is 'tomorrow' (or 'in the future'), ببیند is third-person present/subjunctive 'he will see', and نشیب is a noun 'decline/descent/low point' — so the speaker expresses fear that tomorrow he will witness decline.
segments.s424-g47: همی again is an emphatic adverb 'continually/always'. سوزد is present tense 'burns/aches' (here idiomatic: 'keeps burning'). از marks cause 'from/because of'. مهر means 'love/affection' (the cause of the burning). فرش دلم is a possessive sequence 'the carpet of my heart' (فرش = carpet/ground, دل = heart); the separate particle م listed after دل is here the enclitic possessive '-my' attached to دل, but the provided lemma senses don't include the possessive meaning so it is noted as irregular in the sense list.
segments.s424-g48: ز (poetic variant of از) again marks cause/relation 'from/because of'. فرمان is 'command' and دادار is the epic name/title 'the Creator' (God). دل is 'heart'. نگسلم is a negative-present/future form: ن- prefix for negation + گسلم (from گسستن 'to break') so here 'I will not break' or 'I shall not let it break' — i.e., 'I will not let my heart be broken' because of the Creator's command. The verb is first-person and negated in one fused form, typical of older/persian-poetic morphology.
segments.s424-g57: بخسب is an imperative form meaning “sleep” (tell someone to sleep). امشب و بامداد پگاه is a compound temporal adverbial: امشب “tonight” joined by و “and” to بامداد پگاه, both words meaning dawn/early morning — a poetic doubling for emphasis. The whole segment gives the time when the following command should be carried out.
segments.s424-g58: برو is an imperative “go.” تا here functions in the sense “as far as / up to” (indicating the destination), combined with the preposition به + noun: به ایوان او “to his portico.” بی‌سپاه is a compound with prefix بی- “without” and سپاه “army/guard,” i.e. “unescorted” or “without an army,” modifying (or commenting on the manner of going to) the porch.
segments.s424-g59: بایوان here is a poetic/archaic fused locative (effectively equivalent to به ایوان “at/to the portico”); it is archaic in form. او is the 3rd-person pronoun “his.” روز فرخ کنیم is a volitive/subjunctive construction: روز فرخ “an auspicious/fortunate day” plus کنیم (1st-person-plural subjunctive of کردن) giving the sense “let us make (it) an auspicious day” or “we should make the day auspicious.”
segments.s424-g60: سخن هرچ گویند is a concessive clause: سخن “words/speech,” هرچ “whatever,” and گویند (3rd-person plural present) “they say” → “whatever words they say.” پاسخ کنیم is again the 1st-person-plural subjunctive/optative ‘let us answer’ or ‘we will answer,’ so the whole means “we will answer whatever they say.” Note the verb کنيم expresses intent or resolution here.
segments.s424-g61: همه کار is the subject “all (his) deeds.” نیکوست is a stative predicate (3rd-sg.) meaning “is good” or “is fair.” زو is a poetic contraction of ز + او (= از او) meaning “from him / because of him” and indicates source or cause. در جهان is a locative phrase “in the world.” The word order places the predicate after the noun (typical of Persian) and appends source and locative modifiers.
segments.s424-g62: میان ... و میان ... is a parallel, repetitive prepositional structure meaning “among/between X and among/between Y.” کهان is an archaic word meaning “the world / the heavens” and مهان here means “guests” or “people present” (a collective/poetic usage). The repetition of میان is stylistic for emphasis or parallelism.
segments.s424-g63: همی is an adverb meaning “always / continually” (a habitual marker in poetry). سر نپیچد is a negated verbal phrase: سر “head” + نپیچد (negative present/optative of پیچیدن ‘to turn’) literally “does not turn,” used figuratively for ‘does not rebel/disobey.’ ز فرمان تو = از فرمان تو “from your command” (ز + فرمان + تو) marks the thing he does not turn away from. Overall: “he will not turn his head from your command” i.e. he will not disobey.
segments.s424-g64: دلش راست بینم is a clause with object-before-verb order: دلش “his heart” + راست “true / straight / loyal” + بینم (1st-sg. present of دیدن) “I see.” The speaker asserts that he considers the man’s heart to be loyal. به پیمان تو is a prepositional phrase “to/with your covenant” (faithful to your pledge), modifying راست and explaining in what way the heart is true.
segments.s424-g49: چو here is the subordinating conjunction 'when' (archaic/poetic use), introducing a temporal clause. فردا is the temporal noun 'tomorrow'. بیاید is a present/subjunctive form 'comes'/'should come' referring to that future moment. به + آوردگاه together mark motion/toward a place: 'to the battlefield' (به = towards/into, آوردگاه = battlefield). The clause as a whole is a temporal subordinate clause ('When tomorrow comes to the battlefield').
segments.s424-g50: کنم is first-person singular present/future 'I will do / I will make'. روز روشن is a noun + adjective 'a bright/clear day' (object/result of the verb). بروبر is an adverbial/compound meaning 'face-to-face / directly', and سیاه is an epithet 'the black one' (here a substantive epithet for the enemy or darkness). Word order is Verb (کنم) + object phrase (روز روشن) + manner/adverb (بروبر) + epithet (سیاه).
segments.s424-g51: This is a speech-report clause in past tense: پشوتن is the speaker's name, بدو is the indirect object 'to him', and گفت is the verb 'said' (past). After that follows an imperative: بشنو سخن = 'listen to (these) words' (بشنو imperative 'listen', سخن 'speech/words').
segments.s424-g52: همی here is an emphatic/continuative particle (poetic) attached to the following verb; گویمت is a present/future first-person form 'I tell you' (گویم + enclitic object -ت). ای is the vocative 'O', برادر addresses the addressee 'brother', and مکن is the prohibitive 'do not'. Together this is direct speech: 'I tell you, O brother, do not (do it)'.
segments.s424-g53: ترا here is the object clitic expressed as ت + را (the source text split them); together they mean 'to you'/'you (as definite object)'. گفتم is past 'I said/I told'. و is the coordinating 'and'. بیش 'more' modifies the following verb, گویم is present/future 'I say/I will say' (here promise of additional speech), and همی is a continuative/emphatic particle ('still/still I will say'). The sequence expresses: 'I told you, and I will say still more.'
segments.s424-g54: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that/so that'. از + راستی = 'from/of righteousness/truth'. دل is the subject 'heart(s)'. نشویم is the present/subjunctive first-person-plural negative form 'we do not become / may we not turn' (here 'may our hearts not turn away'). همی again is emphatic/continuative. The clause gives the purpose/result: 'so that our hearts do not depart from righteousness.'
segments.s424-g55: میازار is an imperative negative 'do not harm / do not vex'. کس را = 'anyone' with the object marker را (کس = anyone/someone). که here is the relative 'who/for whom' introducing the following description. آزاد مرد is a fixed phrase 'a free/noble man' (آزاد used in the sense 'noble/generous', مرد in heroic sense). The segment reads: 'Do not harm anyone who is a noble man.'
segments.s424-g56: سر 'head' (literal or metonymic for self/honor) + اندر 'into/within' form the verb phrase with نیارد, which here functions as a negative/resultative verbal idea meaning 'does not put/does not subject' (archaic/poetic construction). The rest (به آزار و درد) is the goal/condition 'to torment and pain'. Together: 'he will not put his head into torment and pain' — i.e. a noble man will not have himself subjected to suffering. (Note: نیارد is an older/poetic form; its exact lemma senses in the provided list do not match and are marked below.)
segments.s424-g65: Literal word order: subject 'تو' + adjunct 'با او' + interrogative 'چه' + verb 'گویی', followed by adverbial phrases 'به کین و به خشم'. 'چه گویی' is an archaic/questional present meaning 'what will you say'. The repeated preposition 'به' ('به کین' / 'به خشم') is a poetic way to mark the manner ('in/with hatred and in/with anger').
segments.s424-g66: 'بشوی' here has an optative/jussive force — a wish or injunction often translated 'may it be washed away' or 'let it be removed'. The phrase that follows supplies the source and the thing removed: 'از دلت' = 'from your heart' (دلت = دل + enclitic ت 'your'), and the postponed noun 'کین' (hatred) is the thing being removed. 'وز' is the poetic contraction of 'و از' ('and from'), so 'وز خشم چشم' = 'and from the eye, anger.' Note the poetic inversion: the verb-like wish comes before the nouns it affects.
segments.s424-g67: The clause has a slightly inverted order: 'یکی پاسخ' ('a certain reply' / 'an answer') + verb with object suffix 'آوردش' ('brought it/him'); the proper subject 'اسفندیار' follows for emphasis. The suffix '-ش' on 'آوردش' stands in for 'to him'.
segments.s424-g68: Relative clause introduced by 'که' ('that') gives the content of the answer. 'بر گوشه گلستان' is a locative prepositional phrase ('on the edge of the rose-garden'), followed by the verb 'رست' (archaic past 'grew'), and finally the subject 'خار' (thorn) appears after the verb, a common poetic order (V ... S).
segments.s424-g69: 'چنین گفت' = 'thus he said' (چنین 'thus', گفت 'said'). The following phrase 'کز مردم پاک‌دین' uses the archaic compound 'کز' (= 'که از' / 'from among') to mean 'from the people/devotees who are pious' — 'مردم' (people) + 'پاک‌دین' (pious/devout).
segments.s424-g70: 'همانا' is an emphatic particle 'indeed/truly'. 'نزیبد' is an archaic present negative-form of 'زیبیدن/زیبد' meaning 'it does not befit' or 'it is not fitting'. The clause 'که گوید چنین' is a subordinate clause introduced by 'که' ('that') with 'گوید' in the general/present-subjunctive sense 'one should say' and 'چنین' 'thus' placed after it — so the whole means 'indeed it does not become (a pious man) that he say thus.'
segments.s424-g71: 'گر' is the conditional 'if'. 'ایدونک' is an archaic/discourse particle here meaning roughly 'thus/indeed' or 'so' (it flags the manner just stated). 'دستور ایران' = 'the custom/order of Iran'; 'توی' = 'in/among' (archaic 'تو + ی' used as locative), so the whole phrase means 'if thus the custom of Iran is among you' or 'if this is Iran's rule among you.'
segments.s424-g72: A catalog of faculties 'دل و گوش و چشم' (heart, ear, and eye) followed by 'دلیران' (your brave ones/warriors) and the locative 'توی' ('in/with you'). The meaning is idiomatic: the hearts, ears and eyes of your warriors are 'with you' (i.e. they follow/are loyal to you). Note the parallel listing and the final locative 'توی' linking them back to 'you'.
segments.s424-g73: همی before the verb gives a continuous/habitual sense ('always, continually'). خوب is an adverb modifying the verb. داری is 2nd-person singular present 'you have/keep/hold' (here 'you pursue/keep'); چنین راه را is the direct object phrase ('such a way') with را as the accusative/object marker. The subject is omitted (you).
segments.s424-g74: This is a coordinated object phrase describing the course referenced earlier. خرد را and شاه را are direct objects marked by را; آزردن is the infinitive 'to hurt/ offend' that applies to those objects (poetic word-order places the infinitive after the coordinator). و simply links the two object-phrases ('and').
segments.s424-g75: همه رنج و تیمار ما is the noun phrase 'all our suffering and care' (ما = our/us). باد گشت is an optative/formulaic construction: باد (optative particle ‘may/let’) + گشت (become) gives the sense 'may all ... be turned' or 'have been turned' (poetic usage often expresses a resulting state). Word order places the theme before the verbal phrase.
segments.s424-g76: همان دین زردشت is the subject phrase 'that very religion of Zoroaster'. بیداد is a noun 'injustice/oppression' and گشت is the past/airy copula 'became'—subject then predicate; 'became injustice' is a simple nominal predicate with گشت as the verbal element.
segments.s424-g77: The initial که گوید که introduces reported speech or a quoted maxim ('which/that says that...'). هر کو is the archaic ‘whoever’/‘anyone who’ (هر = every/any; کو = 'whoever' in older Persian), ز marks separation/fromness ('from' or 'away from'), and فرمان شاه is 'the king's command'. This clause prepares the subordinate condition whose verb comes next.
segments.s424-g78: بپیچد is 3rd-person verb from پیچیدن 'to turn/veer' (here 'turn away / depart'); به دوزخ is the prepositional phrase 'to/into hell'; بود جایگاه places the copula after the locative phrase ('was/is his place')—a poetic word order meaning 'his place will be hell' or 'to hell will be his abode.'
segments.s424-g79: مرا is the direct object 'me'. چند is an interrogative/adverb 'how long / how much longer'. گویی is 2nd-person present 'you say / you keep saying' (here 'you keep calling'); گنهکار is the predicate/complement 'sinful' or 'guilty' that the addressee applies to the speaker. The clause as a whole is a rhetorical question: 'How long will you keep calling me sinful?' (شو here is the archaic/exhortative form 'become' used in the idiomatic framing of the spoken accusation).
segments.s424-g80: ز گفتار گشتاسپ locates the cause/agency ('from/by the speech of Gushtasp' = 'because of Gushtasp's words'). بیزار شو is an imperative: بیزار 'averse, loath' + شو 'become' gives 'be disgusted/turn away'. The preposition ز introduces the ablative/source and گشتاسپ follows گفتار as its possessor.
segments.s424-g33: دل نیک‌مردان is a possessive/construct phrase (the heart(s) of the virtuous); بدو is a prepositional dative pronoun 'to him / toward him'; زنده شد is the two-word predicate 'became alive / revived' with شد as the past/copulative 'became'. Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect: object/possessive phrase + dative + verbal predicate.
segments.s424-g34: بد here is used substantively ('the bad/evil ones', i.e. wicked people) as the sentence subject; از بیم is a causal prepositional phrase 'because of / from fear of'; شمشیر او is a genitive phrase 'his sword'; بنده شد is a copular/ergative construction 'became a servant/slave' with شد as the past 'became'. Overall: 'The wicked, because of fear of his sword, became servants.'
segments.s424-g35: بیامد is the simple past 'came' placed before the subject for poetic inversion. بدر is the proper name (the subject) and پهلوان سوار is an appositive/title meaning 'the (mounted) champion' (پهلوان = champion, سوار = mounted/riding).
segments.s424-g36: پس‌اندر is an adverbial 'afterwards/after that'; همی is the classical narrative/continuous particle that gives immediacy or continuity to the verb; دیدش is the past tense 'saw' with the object clitic -ش 'him' attached; اسفندیار (the subject) follows the verb by poetic inversion. Together: 'Afterwards Esfandiyar saw him.'
segments.s424-g37: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'; برگشت is the past 'returned'; ازو is a source/genitive 'from him/it'; با + پشوتن means 'with Peshotan' (با marking accompaniment or address); and بگفت is the past 'he said' (the reporting verb). The whole segment sets up 'When he returned from him, he said to Peshotan...'.
segments.s424-g38: که introduces reported speech 'that / saying that'; مردی here means 'manliness / brave manliness' (an abstract quality); و links the next noun; گردی in this context denotes a heroic quality (classical usage 'valor/renown') and is the second coordinated noun; نشاید is a negative modal/impersonal 'it is not proper / it cannot be'; نهفت is the passive/adjectival infinitive 'be hidden' (from نهفتن). Together the clause says 'that manliness and valor cannot be concealed.'
segments.s424-g39: ندیدم is the first-person past negative 'I did not see'; بدین گونه is a frozen prepositional phrase 'in this manner / of this sort' (بدین = به + این); اسپ و سوار is the coordinated direct object 'horse and rider' (اسپ = steed, سوار = rider). The whole means 'I have not seen a horse and rider of this kind.'
segments.s424-g40: ندانم is the present negative 'I do not know' (classical present used for the speaker's statement); که introduces a subordinate clause 'how/that'; چون here means 'how' (in what manner); خیزد is the classical 3rd-person present/subjunctive 'he rises / springs up' used to express potential or future action in narrative; از کارزار is a prepositional phrase 'from / in battle' (کارزار = battle). Together: 'I do not know how he will rise in/onto the battlefield.'
segments.s424-g81: تو گویی is a second-person present form ('you say' / 'you may say') followed by و linking the clause. من خود چنین کی کنم is emphatic: من خود ('I myself') + چنین ('thus/like this') + a rhetorical interrogative کی + the present subjunctive/indicative کنم ('what shall I do'). In classical Persian the question-word (کی here rhetorical) precedes the verb; خود stresses the speaker's helplessness.
segments.s424-g82: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/...') giving the content of the complaint. از رای و فرمان او is a prepositional phrase ('by/from his counsel and command') placed before the verb for emphasis; پی کنم is the classical compound verb (پی + کردن) meaning 'to follow' with کنم in the 1st-person present/subjunctive — 'that I follow'.
segments.s424-g83: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک is an emphatic/temporal particle ('even now' / 'as yet'). ترسی is a classical 2nd-person singular present of ترسیدن ('you fear'), and همی is a progressive/continuative particle here ('still/now'). از تنم marks the object of the fear ('for my body' / 'about my life'). Together: 'If you still fear for my body'.
segments.s424-g84: من امروز sets the subject and time ('I, today'). ترس + ت is the noun 'fear' with the 2nd-person possessive suffix (-ت = 'your'), then را marks it as definite/object. بشکنم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive of شکستن used to mean 'I will break/dispel' — 'I will break your fear today.' Word order puts the object before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s424-g85: کسی combined with the negative verb expresses 'no one'. بی‌زمانه is an adverbial/compound meaning 'untimely' or 'before his time'. به گیتی is a locative phrase 'in the world'. نمرد is the past negative of مردن ('did not die'), so the clause reads 'No one died untimely in the world.'
segments.s424-g86: نمرد is repeated as a general statement 'he does not die'. آنک is the relative pronoun 'who/that'. نام بزرگی ببرد is a compact relative clause: نام ('fame/reputation') + بزرگی ('greatness') + ببرد (بردن used in the sense 'to gain/win'); together 'he who wins great fame does not die' — a proverbial statement expressed with present tense for general truth.
segments.s424-g87: تو فردا ببینی uses the 2nd-person present/subjunctive ببینی as a future/predictive ('you will see tomorrow'). که introduces the content clause. بر دشت جنگ is a prepositional locative phrase 'on the plain/field of battle' (بر = on/upon, دشت جنگ = battle-plain).
segments.s424-g88: چه کار آوردَم (چه کار آورم here) is 'what deed I will bring about' — چه کار ('what deed') + آورم (1st-person present/subjunctive 'I bring/bring about'). پیش introduces a locative/face-to-face sense ('before/in front of'); چنگی پلنگ is an ezāfe construction: چنگی ('claw' with ezāfe) + پلنگ ('panther/leopard') = 'the panther's claw(s)'. The whole phrase means 'what I will do facing the panther's claws.'

sentences.s424-s5: When the hero Esfandiyar heard these words, he came on foot to the renowned one.
sentences.s424-s6: He said to Rostam thus: 'O you preoccupied one, why have you come so swiftly to the curtained hall?'
sentences.s424-s7: One who deems it fitting would set on this soil of Zabulistan a body of knowledge called 'Ghalghalestan.'
sentences.s424-s8: For when a guest comes sated from his host, he carries off the steward's name in disgrace.
sentences.s424-s1: When Rostam emerged from the curtained chamber, he stood for a while at the door on foot.
sentences.s424-s2: He said to Kriyas, 'O house of hope — what a fresh day is this in which Jamshid has done you ill.'
sentences.s424-s3: Was it a fortunate or an ill-omened time for Kay Kavus — or that very day of Kay Khosrow the well-born?
sentences.s424-s4: In the noble audience-chamber they now shut you up, for an unworthy one has taken your throne.
sentences.s424-s49: How can I rid my heart of fear in such a way that I can break myself away from thoughts?
sentences.s424-s50: Two fighters, two lions, and two brave men — how should I know which will end up on its back?
sentences.s424-s51: He gave no answer to the famed one; his heart became full of sorrow and his head full of wind.
sentences.s424-s45: Peshotan said to him, 'O renowned one, why do you speak so much about the battlefield?'
sentences.s424-s46: For when you took up bow and arrow, this suspicion of the Devil had not been upon you.
sentences.s424-s47: You have given the devil a way into your heart; now you do not heed the advice of this guide.
sentences.s424-s48: I see your heart bewildered, still full of strife; now whatever I have said has all fallen to pieces.
sentences.s424-s9: Ill-fate said to the inner curtain, 'You once had Jamshid at your side.'
sentences.s424-s10: On that same day, because of King Kavus, the curtain and the palace's canopy/shadow suffered harm.
sentences.s424-s11: Where could one seek the way (path) of God? You strove to set the stars aright.
sentences.s424-s12: Because of him the land was wholly in turmoil; it was full of daggers, plunder, and clubs.
sentences.s424-s13: Now your patron is Gushtasp; in his presence Jamasp is likewise in attendance.
sentences.s424-s14: Zarathushtra sits at his side; he has come from paradise with the Zand.
sentences.s424-s15: Tell the other, Peshotan, O noble man: he has tasted many heats and colds of the world.
sentences.s424-s16: Within the forecourt stands the fortunate Esfandiyar, whose coming made the course of time glad.
sentences.s424-s21: There is a certain ragged elephant on Mount Gung; if he comes into battle with a weapon...
sentences.s424-s22: If he fought with weapons, then truly that would be the code of manliness.
sentences.s424-s23: He will pass upward in splendour and beauty; I fear that tomorrow he will see a decline.
sentences.s424-s24: My heart's carpet continually burns from love; because of the Creator's command I will not break my heart.
sentences.s424-s29: Sleep tonight, and at dawn go to his portico unescorted.
sentences.s424-s30: Let us make the day auspicious at his portico; whatever words they say, let us reply.
sentences.s424-s31: All his deeds are good because of him in the world, between the heavens and among the guests.
sentences.s424-s32: His head will not turn away from your command; I see his heart true to your covenant.
sentences.s424-s25: When tomorrow he comes to the battlefield, I will make the day bright and meet the black one face to face.
sentences.s424-s26: Peshotan said to him, 'Listen to my words — I tell you, O brother, do not (do that).'
sentences.s424-s27: I have told you, and I will say more still — so that our hearts do not turn away from righteousness.
sentences.s424-s28: Harm no one, for a noble man will not have his head subjected to torment and pain.
sentences.s424-s33: What will you say to him in hatred and in anger? May hatred be washed from your heart and anger from your eye.
sentences.s424-s34: Esfandiyar brought him an answer, that a thorn had grown on the edge of the rose-garden.
sentences.s424-s35: Thus he said: it is not fitting for a devout man to speak like this.
sentences.s424-s36: If indeed such is the custom/order of Iran among you, the hearts, ears and eyes of your brave men are with you.
sentences.s424-s37: You always follow such a course: to wound wisdom and to offend the king.
sentences.s424-s38: All our toil and care have been turned aside; that very religion of Zoroaster has become oppression.
sentences.s424-s39: It says that whoever departs from the king's command, to hell will be his place.
sentences.s424-s40: How long will you keep calling me sinful? Be disgusted with the words of Gushtasp.
sentences.s424-s17: The hearts of virtuous men revived toward him; the wicked, out of fear of his sword, became his servants.
sentences.s424-s18: Badr the champion came mounted; afterwards Esfandiyar saw him.
sentences.s424-s19: When he returned from him he spoke with Peshotan, saying that manliness and valor cannot be hidden.
sentences.s424-s20: I have not seen a horse and rider of this sort; I do not know how he will spring up in battle.
sentences.s424-s41: You may say so, but what can I myself do — how could I follow his counsel and command?
sentences.s424-s42: If even now you fear for my body, I will today break your fear.
sentences.s424-s43: No one dies untimely in the world; he does not die who wins great renown.
sentences.s424-s44: Tomorrow you will see what deed I will bring about on the plain of battle before the panther's claws.
